title: Listening - Part 4: Mountain Running
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Claire, who organises mountain-running events. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Claire Morton, who organises mountain-running events. Claire, is mountain running only for very adventurous athletes? || Claire: Not at all. The name makes it sound as though you’re always climbing enormous peaks. In reality, [24]beginners normally start on low hills and short routes before attempting anything more demanding[/24]. It is challenging, but people build up gradually, just as they would in any sport. || Presenter: What about people who live in towns or cities? || Claire: They often assume there’s nowhere to train. However, parks, steps and nearby footpaths are all useful. I live in a city myself and [25]my running club practises on a wooded hill reached by a short bus journey[/25]. You do not need to move to the Scottish Highlands to prepare properly. || Presenter: Mountain races seem to be becoming more visible. Has their character changed? || Claire: They used to be local events connected with village fairs. Some still are, which is lovely, but the sport has grown enormously. [26]Many races now attract competitors from abroad, who plan their holidays around them[/26]. That international interest has brought more funding and safer organisation. || Presenter: You’ve completed the Dragon’s Ridge race. What makes that special? || Claire: It follows a narrow route across several high points and takes almost a full day. People see photographs and want to enter immediately, but they cannot simply register online. [27]Before accepting runners, the organisers require proof that they have completed other difficult mountain events[/27]. On a route like that, experience is a safety issue. || Presenter: Do you think everyone should take part in races? || Claire: No. Some runners love a finish line; others dislike the pressure. [28]A race can give you a goal to train towards, but it isn’t necessary for enjoying mountain running[/28]. I encourage people to try one if they are curious, not because they feel they have to prove something. || Presenter: How does road running compare with mountain running? || Claire: Both are hard in different ways. Road surfaces are flatter, so runners repeat exactly the same movement thousands of times. [29]That repeated impact can cause certain injuries more easily than running on uneven softer ground[/29]. In the mountains, however, you must concentrate on every step. || Presenter: Does an experienced road runner adapt quickly? || Claire: Their fitness gives them a good start, but they can be overconfident. [30]Strong road runners are often surprised by how much slower they need to go on steep, rough paths[/30]. Learning to judge the ground and the weather takes time, even for people who run long distances.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What does Claire say about mountain running?

options.A: New runners can begin on easier routes.
options.B: It is more dangerous than most other sports.
options.C: It is mainly intended for highly trained athletes.

questions.explanation: Claire says beginners usually start on low hills and short routes.

questions.question: People who live in cities

options.A: have to train by using flights of stairs.
options.B: may be able to find suitable hills nearby.
options.C: usually join clubs outside their local area.

questions.explanation: Claire's city club trains on a wooded hill only a short bus journey away.

questions.question: What does Claire say about mountain-running races nowadays?

options.A: They have replaced most village festivals.
options.B: They are usually organised during holidays.
options.C: They may attract runners from different countries.

questions.explanation: She says many races now draw competitors from abroad.

questions.question: The Dragon’s Ridge race

options.A: is only open to runners with relevant experience.
options.B: is shorter than it appears in photographs.
options.C: allows beginners to run with an experienced guide.

questions.explanation: Entrants must show they have completed other difficult mountain events.

questions.question: What does Claire say about taking part in races?

options.A: It is the best way to meet other runners.
options.B: It can encourage training but is not essential.
options.C: It should only be tried after several years.

questions.explanation: She says a race offers a training goal, but people can enjoy the sport without competing.

questions.question: According to Claire, road running

options.A: causes more falls than mountain running.
options.B: is usually less tiring for the body.
options.C: can lead to injuries through repeated impact.

questions.explanation: Claire mentions injuries caused by repeating the same impact on flatter surfaces.

questions.question: Claire says experienced road runners may

options.A: expect to travel faster on mountain paths than is possible.
options.B: find changing weather easier to manage.
options.C: need less training than beginners before racing.

questions.explanation: She says strong road runners are surprised by how much they must slow down on rough, steep routes.
